Q1: I tried really hard to create a structured study routine, dedicating about 24 hours a week to coursework. I would block out time in the mornings and evenings, writing everything down in a planner since I learn best through reading and writing things out. But honestly, even with all that planning, I often felt like I was spinning my wheels—putting in the hours but not seeing the results I wanted. The anxiety made it hard to stick to my schedule sometimes because I'd get overwhelmed just looking at everything I needed to do.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot more than I wanted it to. I'd start each week with good intentions and a set schedule, but then anxiety would hit or I'd fall behind on one thing, and suddenly everything felt chaotic. Deadlines definitely pushed me to cram rather than spread things out evenly. I wanted to be consistent, but my emotions often got in the way of my best-laid plans.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew there was something due soon or when I wanted to check if any new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I wasn't sure what to focus on. The discussion posts and peer interactions also pulled me in—I wanted to stay connected with classmates even when I was struggling.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic, like checking my phone. I'd wake up, make tea, and log in to see what was happening. It felt comforting to have that routine, especially when everything else felt uncertain. But when my anxiety spiked, even that small habit became hard to maintain because opening the platform reminded me of all the things I was behind on.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly a lifesaver—I watched over 250 minutes of them because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion boards were also helpful because seeing other students' perspectives made me feel less alone in my confusion. I appreciated resources I could engage with deeply through reading and note-taking since that's how I process information best.
Q6: I often ignored or delayed the practice quizzes and some of the supplementary readings. Part of it was that I didn't have reliable internet access at home, so anything that required being online for extended periods was difficult. I also avoided things that felt overwhelming when my anxiety was high—if something seemed like it would take too long or be too hard, I'd push it off until it was too late.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection between doing it and understanding the material better. If I finished something and felt like I actually learned something I could write about or explain, that was valuable. But if it felt like busywork or if I couldn't see how it connected to the bigger picture, my motivation dropped quickly, especially when I was already anxious about everything else.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. On one hand, I knew they could help me learn more, but on the other hand, I was already struggling to complete the required work. When I did optional activities, I often felt guilty because I thought I should be focusing on assignments instead. Required activities at least had the pressure of deadlines pushing me forward, even if that pressure added to my anxiety.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I did a lot of studying offline because I don't have consistent internet access. I would download PDFs, print out readings, and take extensive handwritten notes. I have a whole notebook filled with summaries and concepts that I worked through on my own. My best learning happened with a pen in my hand, away from screens, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with my printed notes and textbook for hours, really wrestling with concepts and rewriting explanations until they made sense to me. I'd be learning intensely, but since I wasn't online, there's no record of it. Also, I studied with a few classmates in person sometimes—we'd work through problems together—but that kind of collaboration doesn't show up in any log.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF during the brief windows when I had internet access, and then I'd study from those files for days afterward. One video might show as a single view, but I actually watched it multiple times on my laptop offline, pausing to take notes. The LMS only captured that initial download, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: Midway through the semester, things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started feeling sad most of the time. Even though I was putting in hours, my assignment completion dropped because I couldn't bring myself to submit work that didn't feel perfect. The CBT sessions helped me recognize some of these patterns, but by then I was already so far behind that catching up felt impossible.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also ate into my study time, even though I tried to balance everything. The confusion about what to prioritize made me freeze up sometimes—I'd spend an hour deciding what to work on instead of actually working.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open readings or start watching videos, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about exams, thinking about everything I hadn't finished. I'd reach the end of a page and realize I hadn't absorbed a single word. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I kept doing them because at least I was trying.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even the ones I knew were crucial. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd think about all the ways I could fail, and that paralyzed me. Sometimes I avoided things because I was afraid my work wouldn't be good enough, so not submitting felt safer than submitting something imperfect. My assignment completion rate really suffered because of this.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my only motivator. Without them, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all, but they also caused intense anxiety spikes. I'd often wait until the last possible moment, then rush through things in a panic. The LMS would show me logging in right before deadlines, which looks like procrastination, but really it was fear holding me back until I had no choice.
Q17: Feedback affected me deeply, maybe too deeply. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but any criticism sent me spiraling. After getting a low exam score, I found it hard to engage with the course for days because I felt like a failure. The feedback was probably meant to help me improve, but my anxiety turned it into evidence that I wasn't good enough.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which was both good and bad. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to follow, but there wasn't much flexibility for someone like me who was struggling emotionally. I wished there were more check-ins or alternative pathways when I fell behind. Once I missed a few things, it felt like the structure was working against me rather than supporting me.
Q19: The heavy reliance on online engagement made things harder since I don't have reliable internet at home. Also, the pace of new content being released didn't account for students who needed more time to process. On the positive side, having recorded lectures I could download was incredibly helpful. Discussion boards were accessible and made me feel connected even when I was struggling alone.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was highly engaged with the LMS—watching videos, participating in discussions, interacting with peers—but my assignment completion was low. For me, it was anxiety and perfectionism. I was learning and trying, but the final step of submitting work felt terrifying. The activity was genuine engagement; the missing submissions were about fear, not laziness.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some students might be really efficient—they download what they need, study offline, and just submit their work without spending hours browsing the LMS. If someone has good resources and a clear study method, they don't need to be logged in constantly. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low effort; it might just mean different study habits.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't capture whether you actually understood something or just scrolled past it. They definitely can't show emotional states, like how anxiety might make someone spend hours on the platform without accomplishing anything meaningful.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about learning the material and actively trying to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present, asking questions, making connections. For me, engagement also means persistence—showing up even when it's hard, even when anxiety tells me to give up. It's not just about what you do; it's about how invested you are in your own learning.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting against my own anxiety to participate at all. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline with printed materials, or the emotional toll of trying to keep up while feeling like I was drowning. It doesn't show that I cared deeply about this course and wanted to succeed—my failing grade doesn't reflect my effort or my learning, just my inability to submit work on time.
